revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Miyazu-hime]][[ja:宮簀媛]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11455216]]
Wikidata name: Miyazu-hime
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Short description|Japanese noblewoman and goddess}}

{{infobox deity
| type = Shinto
| father = {{ill|Otoyo no mikoto|ja|乎止与命|complete=true|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otoyo no mikoto|qq=}}
| cult_centre = [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]]
| cult_center = [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]]
| spouse = [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]]
| gender = {{ill|female|qid=Q6581072}}
| parents = {{ill|Otoyo no mikoto|qid=Q97706258}}
| consort = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|qid=Q461258}}
}}

'''Miyazu-hime''' the wife of [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] was a member of the [[Owari clan]].<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016">{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=English |chapter=Atsuta Jingu}}</ref> She is a kami of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]].<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429" />

According to legend she is buried in [[Danpusan Kofun]].<ref name="Shiseki2">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>

The [[Owari clan]] is a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]. The clan were originally [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] but after the abolition of the role they took on a priestly role at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], they share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref>https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].

The "Daijingu engi" records show that during Emperor Temmu's time, the divine sword was returned to the palace. Seven guardians were then appointed to oversee its worship. These guardians were linked to Miyazu-hime and Takeinatane.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />

According to traditional sources, [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] died in the 43rd year of Emperor Keiko's reign (景行天皇43rd year , equivalent 113 AD).<ref name="shrines433">Ponsonby-Fane, p. 433.</ref>  The possessions of the dead prince were gathered together along with the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]; and his widow Miyazu-hime venerated his memory in a shrine at her home.  Sometime later, these relics and the sacred sword were moved to the current location of the Atsuta Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 434.</ref>

''Nihonshoki'' explains that this move occurred in the 51st year of Keiko's reign, but shrine tradition also dates this event in the 1st year of Emperor Chūai's reign.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 435.</ref>
The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in 192, and held the position of the shrine's high priest since ancient times, passing it down from generation to generation.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Naito |first=Toho |title=Choshu Zasshi (張州雑志) |publisher=Aichi-ken Kyōdo Shiryō Kankō-kai |year=1975 |doi=10.11501/9537297}}</ref> [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] and Miyazu-hime and her brother {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}} are worshipped at the shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429">Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429.</ref>

When Miyazu-hime died, a shrine was set up in her honor in Hikami. Her clan members became priests there. Inatane, the eleventh descendant of Amenohoakari, founded the Owari clan. As a result, Atsuta Myojin became the Owari clan's Ujigami.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />

The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyazu-hime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />

== Family tree ==
{{Generations of Jimmu|draft}}
==Notelist==
{{notelist}}

==References==
{{reflist}}

{{Atsuta Faith}}

The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyazu-hime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />

'''Miyazu-hime''' the wife of [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] was a member of the [[Owari clan]].<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016">{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=English |chapter=Atsuta Jingu}}</ref> She is a kami of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]].<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429" />

According to legend she is buried in [[Danpusan Kofun]].<ref name="Shiseki2">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>

The [[Owari clan]] is a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]. The clan were originally [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] but after the abolition of the role they took on a priestly role at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], they share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref>https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].

The "Daijingu engi" records show that during Emperor Temmu's time, the divine sword was returned to the palace. Seven guardians were then appointed to oversee its worship. These guardians were linked to Miyazu-hime and Takeinatane.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />

According to traditional sources, [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] died in the 43rd year of Emperor Keiko's reign (景行天皇43rd year , equivalent 113 AD).<ref name="shrines433">Ponsonby-Fane, p. 433.</ref>  The possessions of the dead prince were gathered together along with the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]; and his widow Miyazu-hime venerated his memory in a shrine at her home.  Sometime later, these relics and the sacred sword were moved to the current location of the Atsuta Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 434.</ref>

''Nihonshoki'' explains that this move occurred in the 51st year of Keiko's reign, but shrine tradition also dates this event in the 1st year of Emperor Chūai's reign.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 435.</ref>
The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in 192, and held the position of the shrine's high priest since ancient times, passing it down from generation to generation.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Naito |first=Toho |title=Choshu Zasshi (張州雑志) |publisher=Aichi-ken Kyōdo Shiryō Kankō-kai |year=1975 |doi=10.11501/9537297}}</ref> [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] and Miyazu-hime and her brother {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}} are worshipped at the shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429">Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429.</ref>

When Miyazu-hime died, a shrine was set up in her honor in Hikami. Her clan members became priests there. Inatane, the eleventh descendant of Amenohoakari, founded the Owari clan. As a result, Atsuta Myojin became the Owari clan's Ujigami.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />

The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyazu-hime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />

'''Miyazu-hime''' the wife of [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] was a member of the [[Owari clan]].<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016">{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=English |chapter=Atsuta Jingu}}</ref> She is a kami of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]].<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429" />

According to legend she is buried in [[Danpusan Kofun]].<ref name="Shiseki2">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>

The [[Owari clan]] is a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]. The clan were originally [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] but after the abolition of the role they took on a priestly role at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], they share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref>https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].

The "Daijingu engi" records show that during Emperor Temmu's time, the divine sword was returned to the palace. Seven guardians were then appointed to oversee its worship. These guardians were linked to Miyazu-hime and Takeinatane.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />

According to traditional sources, [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] died in the 43rd year of Emperor Keiko's reign (景行天皇43rd year , equivalent 113 AD).<ref name="shrines433">Ponsonby-Fane, p. 433.</ref>  The possessions of the dead prince were gathered together along with the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]; and his widow Miyazu-hime venerated his memory in a shrine at her home.  Sometime later, these relics and the sacred sword were moved to the current location of the Atsuta Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 434.</ref>

''Nihonshoki'' explains that this move occurred in the 51st year of Keiko's reign, but shrine tradition also dates this event in the 1st year of Emperor Chūai's reign.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 435.</ref>
The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in 192, and held the position of the shrine's high priest since ancient times, passing it down from generation to generation.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Naito |first=Toho |title=Choshu Zasshi (張州雑志) |publisher=Aichi-ken Kyōdo Shiryō Kankō-kai |year=1975 |doi=10.11501/9537297}}</ref> [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] and Miyazu-hime and her brother {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}} are worshipped at the shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429">Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429.</ref>

When Miyazu-hime died, a shrine was set up in her honor in Hikami. Her clan members became priests there. Inatane, the eleventh descendant of Amenohoakari, founded the Owari clan. As a result, Atsuta Myojin became the Owari clan's Ujigami.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />

The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyazu-hime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />

According to traditional sources, [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] died in the 43rd year of Emperor Keiko's reign (景行天皇43rd year , equivalent 113 AD).<ref name="shrines433">Ponsonby-Fane, p. 433.</ref> The possessions of the dead prince were gathered together along with the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]; and his widow Miyazu-hime venerated his memory in a shrine at her home. Sometime later, these relics and the sacred sword were moved to the current location of the Atsuta Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 434.</ref>
''Nihonshoki'' explains that this move occurred in the 51st year of Keiko's reign, but shrine tradition also dates this event in the 1st year of Emperor Chūai's reign.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 435.</ref> The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in 192, and held the position of the shrine's high priest since ancient times, passing it down from generation to generation.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Naito |first=Toho |title=Choshu Zasshi (張州雑志) |publisher=Aichi-ken Kyōdo Shiryō Kankō-kai |year=1975 |doi=10.11501/9537297}}</ref> [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] and Miyazu-hime and her brother {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}} are worshipped at the shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429">Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429.</ref>
When Miyazu-hime died, a shrine was set up in her honor in Hikami. Her clan members became priests there. Inatane, the eleventh descendant of Amenohoakari, founded the Owari clan. As a result, Atsuta Myojin became the Owari clan's Ujigami.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyazu-hime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />

The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyazu-hime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />

{{Short description|Japanese noblewoman and goddess}}

{{infobox deity
| type = Shinto
| father = {{ill|Otoyo no mikoto|ja|乎止与命|complete=true|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otoyo no mikoto|qq=}}
| cult_centre = [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]]
| cult_center = [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]]
| spouse = [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]]
| gender = {{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}
| parents = {{ill|Otoyo no mikoto|WD=Q97706258}}
| consort = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}}
}}

'''Miyazu-hime''' the wife of [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] was a member of the [[Owari clan]].<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016">{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=English |chapter=Atsuta Jingu}}</ref> She is a kami of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]].<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429" />

According to legend she is buried in [[Danpusan Kofun]].<ref name="Shiseki2">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>

The [[Owari clan]] is a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]. The clan were originally [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] but after the abolition of the role they took on a priestly role at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], they share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref>https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].

The "Daijingu engi" records show that during Emperor Temmu's time, the divine sword was returned to the palace. Seven guardians were then appointed to oversee its worship. These guardians were linked to Miyazu-hime and Takeinatane.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />

According to traditional sources, [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] died in the 43rd year of Emperor Keiko's reign (景行天皇43rd year , equivalent 113 AD).<ref name="shrines433">Ponsonby-Fane, p. 433.</ref>  The possessions of the dead prince were gathered together along with the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]; and his widow Miyazu-hime venerated his memory in a shrine at her home.  Sometime later, these relics and the sacred sword were moved to the current location of the Atsuta Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 434.</ref>

''Nihonshoki'' explains that this move occurred in the 51st year of Keiko's reign, but shrine tradition also dates this event in the 1st year of Emperor Chūai's reign.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 435.</ref>
The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in 192, and held the position of the shrine's high priest since ancient times, passing it down from generation to generation.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Naito |first=Toho |title=Choshu Zasshi (張州雑志) |publisher=Aichi-ken Kyōdo Shiryō Kankō-kai |year=1975 |doi=10.11501/9537297}}</ref> [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] and Miyazu-hime and her brother {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}} are worshipped at the shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429">Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429.</ref>

When Miyazu-hime died, a shrine was set up in her honor in Hikami. Her clan members became priests there. Inatane, the eleventh descendant of Amenohoakari, founded the Owari clan. As a result, Atsuta Myojin became the Owari clan's Ujigami.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />

The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyazu-hime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />

{{infobox deity | type = Shinto | father = {{ill|Otoyo no mikoto|ja|乎止与命|complete=true|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otoyo no mikoto|qq=}} | cult_centre = [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] | cult_center = [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] | spouse = [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] | gender = {{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}} | parents = {{ill|Otoyo no mikoto|WD=Q97706258}} | consort = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} }}
'''Miyazu-hime''' the wife of [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] was a member of the [[Owari clan]].<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016">{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=English |chapter=Atsuta Jingu}}</ref> She is a kami of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]].<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429" />
According to legend she is buried in [[Danpusan Kofun]].<ref name="Shiseki2">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The [[Owari clan]] is a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]. The clan were originally [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] but after the abolition of the role they took on a priestly role at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], they share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref>https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].
The "Daijingu engi" records show that during Emperor Temmu's time, the divine sword was returned to the palace. Seven guardians were then appointed to oversee its worship. These guardians were linked to Miyazu-hime and Takeinatane.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
According to traditional sources, [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] died in the 43rd year of Emperor Keiko's reign (景行天皇43rd year , equivalent 113 AD).<ref name="shrines433">Ponsonby-Fane, p. 433.</ref> The possessions of the dead prince were gathered together along with the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]; and his widow Miyazu-hime venerated his memory in a shrine at her home. Sometime later, these relics and the sacred sword were moved to the current location of the Atsuta Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 434.</ref>
''Nihonshoki'' explains that this move occurred in the 51st year of Keiko's reign, but shrine tradition also dates this event in the 1st year of Emperor Chūai's reign.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 435.</ref> The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in 192, and held the position of the shrine's high priest since ancient times, passing it down from generation to generation.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Naito |first=Toho |title=Choshu Zasshi (張州雑志) |publisher=Aichi-ken Kyōdo Shiryō Kankō-kai |year=1975 |doi=10.11501/9537297}}</ref> [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] and Miyazu-hime and her brother {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}} are worshipped at the shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429">Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429.</ref>
When Miyazu-hime died, a shrine was set up in her honor in Hikami. Her clan members became priests there. Inatane, the eleventh descendant of Amenohoakari, founded the Owari clan. As a result, Atsuta Myojin became the Owari clan's Ujigami.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyazu-hime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />

The [[Owari clan]] is a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]. The clan were originally [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] but after the abolition of the role they took on a priestly role at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], they share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref>https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].
The "Daijingu engi" records show that during Emperor Temmu's time, the divine sword was returned to the palace. Seven guardians were then appointed to oversee its worship. These guardians were linked to Miyazu-hime and Takeinatane.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
According to traditional sources, [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] died in the 43rd year of Emperor Keiko's reign (景行天皇43rd year , equivalent 113 AD).<ref name="shrines433">Ponsonby-Fane, p. 433.</ref> The possessions of the dead prince were gathered together along with the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]; and his widow Miyazu-hime venerated his memory in a shrine at her home. Sometime later, these relics and the sacred sword were moved to the current location of the Atsuta Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 434.</ref>
''Nihonshoki'' explains that this move occurred in the 51st year of Keiko's reign, but shrine tradition also dates this event in the 1st year of Emperor Chūai's reign.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 435.</ref> The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in 192, and held the position of the shrine's high priest since ancient times, passing it down from generation to generation.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Naito |first=Toho |title=Choshu Zasshi (張州雑志) |publisher=Aichi-ken Kyōdo Shiryō Kankō-kai |year=1975 |doi=10.11501/9537297}}</ref> [[:en:Yamato Takeru|Yamato Takeru]] and Miyazu-hime and her brother {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}} are worshipped at the shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429">Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429.</ref>
When Miyazu-hime died, a shrine was set up in her honor in Hikami. Her clan members became priests there. Inatane, the eleventh descendant of Amenohoakari, founded the Owari clan. As a result, Atsuta Myojin became the Owari clan's Ujigami.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyazu-hime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />

B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=English |chapter=Atsuta Jingu}}</ref> She is a kami of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]].<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429" />
According to legend she is buried in [[Danpusan Kofun]].<ref name="Shiseki2">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>

When Miyazu-hime died, a shrine was set up in her honor in Hikami. Her clan members became priests there. Inatane, the eleventh descendant of Amenohoakari, founded the Owari clan. As a result, Atsuta Myojin became the Owari clan's Ujigami.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyazu-hime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Miyazu-hime]][[ja:宮簀媛]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Miyazu-hime]][[ja:宮簀媛]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Miyazu-hime]][[ja:宮簀媛]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11455216]] Wikidata name: Miyazu-hime [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Japanese noblewoman and goddess}}
{{infobox deity | type = Shinto | father = {{ill|Otoyo no mikoto|ja|乎止与命|complete=true|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otoyo no mikoto|qq=}} | cult_centre = [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] | cult_center = [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] | spouse = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} | gender = {{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}} | parents = {{ill|Otoyo no mikoto|WD=Q97706258}} | consort = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} }}
'''Miyazu-hime''' the wife of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} was a member of the [[Owari clan]].<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016">{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=English |chapter=Atsuta Jingu}}</ref> She is a kami of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]].<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429" />
According to legend she is buried in [[Danpusan Kofun]].<ref name="Shiseki2">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The [[Owari clan]] is a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]. The clan were originally [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] but after the abolition of the role they took on a priestly role at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], they share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref>https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].
The "Daijingu engi" records show that during Emperor Temmu's time, the divine sword was returned to the palace. Seven guardians were then appointed to oversee its worship. These guardians were linked to Miyazu-hime and Takeinatane.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
According to traditional sources, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} died in the 43rd year of Emperor Keiko's reign (景行天皇43rd year , equivalent 113 AD).<ref name="shrines433">Ponsonby-Fane, p. 433.</ref> The possessions of the dead prince were gathered together along with the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]; and his widow Miyazu-hime venerated his memory in a shrine at her home. Sometime later, these relics and the sacred sword were moved to the current location of the Atsuta Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 434.</ref>
''Nihonshoki'' explains that this move occurred in the 51st year of Keiko's reign, but shrine tradition also dates this event in the 1st year of Emperor Chūai's reign.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 435.</ref> The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in 192, and held the position of the shrine's high priest since ancient times, passing it down from generation to generation.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Naito |first=Toho |title=Choshu Zasshi (張州雑志) |publisher=Aichi-ken Kyōdo Shiryō Kankō-kai |year=1975 |doi=10.11501/9537297}}</ref> {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru}} and Miyazu-hime and her brother {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}} are worshipped at the shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429">Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429.</ref>
When Miyazu-hime died, a shrine was set up in her honor in Hikami. Her clan members became priests there. Inatane, the eleventh descendant of Amenohoakari, founded the Owari clan. As a result, Atsuta Myojin became the Owari clan's Ujigami.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyazu-hime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />

{{Short description|Japanese noblewoman and goddess}}
{{infobox deity | type = Shinto | father = {{ill|Otoyo no mikoto|ja|乎止与命|complete=true|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otoyo no mikoto|qq=}} | cult_centre = [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] | cult_center = [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] | spouse = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} | gender = {{ill|female|WD=Q6581072|1=Female (gender)}} | parents = {{ill|Otoyo no mikoto|WD=Q97706258|1=Otoyo no mikoto}} | consort = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|1=Yamato Takeru}} }}
'''Miyazu-hime''' the wife of {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} was a member of the [[Owari clan]].<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016">{{Cite book |last=Ponsonby-Fane |first=R. A. B. |title=Studies In Shinto & Shrines |date=2016-05-11 |publisher=Routledge |isbn=978-1-138-98322-9 |edition=1st |language=English |chapter=Atsuta Jingu}}</ref> She is a kami of [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]].<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429" />
According to legend she is buried in [[Danpusan Kofun]].<ref name="Shiseki2">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The [[Owari clan]] is a [[Japanese clans|Japanese clan]]. The clan were originally [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] but after the abolition of the role they took on a priestly role at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]], they share this history with the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏|1=Munakata clan|fr|Clan Munakata|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Munakata clan|qq=}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],<ref>https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839</ref> and the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Yamato clan]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]].
The "Daijingu engi" records show that during Emperor Temmu's time, the divine sword was returned to the palace. Seven guardians were then appointed to oversee its worship. These guardians were linked to Miyazu-hime and Takeinatane.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
According to traditional sources, {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} died in the 43rd year of Emperor Keiko's reign (景行天皇43rd year , equivalent 113 AD).<ref name="shrines433">Ponsonby-Fane, p. 433.</ref> The possessions of the dead prince were gathered together along with the sword [[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi]]; and his widow Miyazu-hime venerated his memory in a shrine at her home. Sometime later, these relics and the sacred sword were moved to the current location of the Atsuta Shrine.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 434.</ref>
''Nihonshoki'' explains that this move occurred in the 51st year of Keiko's reign, but shrine tradition also dates this event in the 1st year of Emperor Chūai's reign.<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, p. 435.</ref> The Owari clan established the [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in 192, and held the position of the shrine's high priest since ancient times, passing it down from generation to generation.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Naito |first=Toho |title=Choshu Zasshi (張州雑志) |publisher=Aichi-ken Kyōdo Shiryō Kankō-kai |year=1975 |doi=10.11501/9537297}}</ref> {{ill|Yamato Takeru|en|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru|1=Yamato Takeru}} and Miyazu-hime and her brother {{ill|Takeinadane|ja|建稲種命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeinadane|qq=}} are worshipped at the shrine.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429">Ponsonby-Fane, p. 429.</ref>
When Miyazu-hime died, a shrine was set up in her honor in Hikami. Her clan members became priests there. Inatane, the eleventh descendant of Amenohoakari, founded the Owari clan. As a result, Atsuta Myojin became the Owari clan's Ujigami.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
The shrine, originally a Betsugu, later became a Sessha. It was founded in the fourth year of Chtiai (195), about 80 years after Yamato Takeru no Mikoto's death. Miyazu-hime was young when she knew Yamato Takeru no Mikoto.<ref name="Ponsonby-Fane-2016" />
== Family tree == {{Generations of Jimmu|draft}} ==Notelist== {{notelist}}
